content: Я вот хочу, чтобы другой аккаунт Cloud Code работал с теми же тоже мой второй, работал с теми же вот наработками, что в этом Cloud Code. Как это сделать? С теми же проектами, с теми же чатами. Если я просто переключу в приложение, то там не будет этих чатов последних, допустим. Ну вообще любых. Как это правильнее сделать?

message.content: Я вот хочу, чтобы другой аккаунт Cloud Code работал с теми же тоже мой второй, работал с теми же вот наработками, что в этом Cloud Code. Как это сделать? С теми же проектами, с теми же чатами. Если я просто переключу в приложение, то там не будет этих чатов последних, допустим. Ну вообще любых. Как это правильнее сделать?

addedLines: - claude: Catch-all for any task that doesn't fit a more specific agent. FleetView's default when no agent name is typed. (Tools: *)

- claude-code-guide: Use this agent when the user asks questions ("Can Claude...", "Does Claude...", "How do I...") about: (1) Claude Code (the CLI tool) - features, hooks, slash commands, MCP servers, settings, IDE integrations, keyboard shortcuts; (2) Claude Agent SDK - building custom agents; (3) Claude API (formerly Anthropic API) - Messages API for directly passing messages to Claude, Tool Runner (`client.beta.messages.tool_runner`) for running an agentic loop over your own tools, manual tool-use loops, Managed Agents for server-hosted agents with a managed sandbox, prompt caching, and general Anthropic SDK usage; (4) Claude Tag (Claude in Slack) - what it is, setting it up for a Slack workspace, `/install-slack-app`. **IMPORTANT:** Before spawning a new agent, check if there is already a running or recently completed claude-code-guide agent that you can continue via SendMessage. (Tools: Glob, Grep, Read, WebFetch, WebSearch)

- Explore: Read-only search agent for broad fan-out searches — when answering means sweeping many files, directories, or naming conventions and you only need the conclusion, not the file dumps. It reads excerpts rather than whole files, so it locates code; it doesn't review or audit it. Specify search breadth: "medium" for moderate exploration, "very thorough" for multiple locations and naming conventions. (Tools: All tools except Agent, Artifact, ExitPlanMode, Edit, Write, NotebookEdit)

- general-purpose: General-purpose agent for researching complex questions, searching for code, and executing multi-step tasks. When you are searching for a keyword or file and are not confident that you will find the right match in the first few tries use this agent to perform the search for you. (Tools: *)

- Plan: Software architect agent for designing implementation plans. Use this when you need to plan the implementation strategy for a task. Returns step-by-step plans, identifies critical files, and considers architectural trade-offs. (Tools: All tools except Agent, Artifact, ExitPlanMode, Edit, Write, NotebookEdit)
- statusline-setup: Use this agent to configure the user's Claude Code status line setting. (Tools: Read, Edit)

attachment.content:
- session-start-hook: Creating and developing startup hooks for Claude Code on the web. Use when the user wants to set up a repository for Claude Code on the web, create a SessionStart hook to ensure their project can run tests and linters during web sessions.
- deep-research: Deep research harness — fan-out web searches, fetch sources, adversarially verify claims, synthesize a cited report. - When the user wants a deep, multi-source, fact-checked research report on any topic. BEFORE invoking, check if the question is specific enough to research directly — if underspecified (e.g., "what car to buy" without budget/use-case/region), ask 2-3 clarifying questions to narrow scope. Then pass the refined question as args, weaving the answers in.
- dataviz: Use this skill whenever you are about to create ANY chart, graph, plot, dashboard, or data visualization, in ANY output medium — an HTML or React artifact, inline SVG, plotting code in any library (matplotlib, plotly, d3, Recharts, …), an image/PNG you will render and upload, or a chart shared into Slack. Read it BEFORE writing the first line of chart code, choosing chart colors, building a stat tile / meter / KPI row, or laying out a dashboard. Produces visualizations that read as one system — elegant, accessible, consistent in light and dark — using a brand-neutral placeholder palette you swap for your own. Teaches a design-system-agnostic method: a form heuristic, a color formula with a runnable validator, mark specs, and interaction rules. A validated default palette is documented in `references/palette.md` — swap that file's values for your brand's. Triggers on: "chart", "graph", "plot", "data viz", "visualization", "dashboard", "analytics", "visualize data", "categorical colors", "sequential / diverging palette", "stat tile", "sparkline", "heatmap", "legend", "axis", "tooltip", "chart colors", "color by series".
- artifact-design: Design guidance and fundamentals for Artifacts.
- update-config: Use this skill to configure the Claude Code harness via settings.json. Automated behaviors ("from now on when X", "each time X", "whenever X", "before/after X") require hooks configured in settings.json - the harness executes these, not Claude, so memory/preferences cannot fulfill them. Also use for: permissions ("allow X", "add permission", "move permission to"), env vars ("set X=Y"), hook troubleshooting, or any changes to settings.json/settings.local.json files. Examples: "allow npm commands", "add bq permission to global settings", "move permission to user settings", "set DEBUG=true", "when claude stops show X". For simple settings like theme/model, suggest the /config command.
- keybindings-help: Use when the user wants to customize keyboard shortcuts, rebind keys, add chord bindings, or modify ~/.claude/keybindings.json. Examples: "rebind ctrl+s", "add a chord shortcut", "change the submit key", "customize keybindings".
- verify: Verify that a code change actually does what it's supposed to by exercising it end-to-end and observing behavior — drive the affected flow, not just tests or typecheck. Run before committing nontrivial changes; bootstraps this repo's project verify skill if none exists yet. Don't invoke it on a diff that only touches tests, docs, or other code with no runtime surface to drive (a change to product source always has one) — there's nothing to observe.
- code-review: Review the current diff for correctness bugs and reuse/simplification/efficiency cleanups at the given effort level (low/medium: fewer, high-confidence findings; high→max: broader coverage, may include uncertain findings). Pass --comment to post findings as inline PR comments, or --fix to apply the findings to the working tree after the review.
- simplify: Review the changed code for reuse, simplification, efficiency, and altitude cleanups, then apply the fixes. Quality only — it does not hunt for bugs; use /code-review for that.
- fewer-permission-prompts: Scan your transcripts for common read-only Bash and MCP tool calls, then add a prioritized allowlist to project .claude/settings.json to reduce permission prompts.
- loop: Run a prompt or slash command on a recurring interval (e.g. /loop 5m /foo, defaults to 10m) - When the user wants to set up a recurring task, poll for status, or run something repeatedly on an interval (e.g. "check the deploy every 5 minutes", "keep running /babysit-prs"). Do NOT invoke for one-off tasks.
- claude-api: Reference for the Claude API / Anthropic SDK — model ids, pricing, params, streaming, tool use, MCP, agents, caching, token counting, model migration.
TRIGGER — read BEFORE opening the target file; don't skip because it "looks like a one-liner" — whenever: the prompt names Claude/Anthropic in any form (Claude, Anthropic, Fable, Opus, Sonnet, Haiku, `anthropic`, `@anthropic-ai`, `claude-*`, `us.anthropic.*`, `[1m]`); the user asks about an LLM (pricing/model choice/limits/caching) — never answer from memory; OR the task is LLM-shaped with provider unstated (agent/MCP/tool-definition/multi-agent/RAG/LLM-judge/computer-use; generate/summarize/extract/classify/rewrite/converse over NL; debugging refusals/cutoffs/streaming/tool-calls/tokens).
SKIP only when another provider is being worked on (overrides all triggers): OpenAI/GPT/Gemini/Llama/Mistral/Cohere/Ollama named in the query; OR `grep -rE 'openai|langchain_openai|google.generativeai|genai|mistralai|cohere|ollama'` over the project hits (run this grep FIRST if no provider named — don't Read the file).
- run: Launch and drive this project's app to see a change working. Use when asked to run, start, or screenshot the app, or to confirm a change works in the real app (not just tests). First looks for a project skill that already covers launching the app; otherwise falls back to built-in patterns per project type (CLI, server, TUI, Electron, browser-driven, library).
- init: Initialize a new CLAUDE.md file with codebase documentation
- review: Review a GitHub pull request; for your working diff use /code-review
- security-review: Complete a security review of the pending changes on the current branch

content.text: Хороший вопрос — давайте я сначала уточню, как именно устроены аккаунты, чаты и проекты в Claude Code, чтобы дать точный ответ, а не догадку.

input.prompt:
I need accurate, current information about how Claude Code (specifically "Claude Code on the web" at claude.ai/code, but also the CLI/desktop/mobile apps) handles accounts, chat/session history, and projects. The user wants to know: can a SECOND, separate Claude account access the SAME chats/sessions and the same projects that were created under a FIRST account?

Please research and answer these specific questions:

1. Are Claude Code sessions/chats tied to an individual user account, or can they be shared across accounts?
2. Is there any feature for "account sharing" or transferring session/chat history between two different Claude accounts?
3. How do Teams/Organization plans work — if two users are in the same Claude Team/org, can they see each other's Claude Code sessions/chats? Or are sessions still private per user?
4. How are "projects" (git repositories/environments) associated in Claude Code on the web? Are environments tied to a user or shareable?
5. What is the realistic, officially-supported way for someone to have their work (projects + chat history) available across two accounts they own?
6. Does git/GitHub play a role here — i.e., is the actual persisted work in the git repo (committed + pushed) rather than in the chat session?

Consult the official docs at https://code.claude.com/docs/en/claude-code-on-the-web and related pages (https://code.claude.com/docs). Please give me concrete, cited findings with URLs. Be honest about what is and isn't possible; do not invent features.

toolUseResult.prompt:
I need accurate, current information about how Claude Code (specifically "Claude Code on the web" at claude.ai/code, but also the CLI/desktop/mobile apps) handles accounts, chat/session history, and projects. The user wants to know: can a SECOND, separate Claude account access the SAME chats/sessions and the same projects that were created under a FIRST account?

Please research and answer these specific questions:

1. Are Claude Code sessions/chats tied to an individual user account, or can they be shared across accounts?
2. Is there any feature for "account sharing" or transferring session/chat history between two different Claude accounts?
3. How do Teams/Organization plans work — if two users are in the same Claude Team/org, can they see each other's Claude Code sessions/chats? Or are sessions still private per user?
4. How are "projects" (git repositories/environments) associated in Claude Code on the web? Are environments tied to a user or shareable?
5. What is the realistic, officially-supported way for someone to have their work (projects + chat history) available across two accounts they own?
6. Does git/GitHub play a role here — i.e., is the actual persisted work in the git repo (committed + pushed) rather than in the chat session?

Consult the official docs at https://code.claude.com/docs/en/claude-code-on-the-web and related pages (https://code.claude.com/docs). Please give me concrete, cited findings with URLs. Be honest about what is and isn't possible; do not invent features.

<task-id>a46dbe8f11e95fb71</task-id>
<tool-use-id>toolu_01CvsBAHuBrLRwZ58GDeczAR</tool-use-id>
<output-file>/tmp/claude-0/-home-user-panamera-studio/c810ce71-594c-5291-94ff-5a2cc7ccbec0/tasks/a46dbe8f11e95fb71.output</output-file>
<status>completed</status>
<summary>Agent "Research Claude Code account/session sharing" finished</summary>
<note>A task-notification fires each time this agent stops with no live background children of its own. The user can send it another message and resume it, so the same task-id may notify more than once.</note>
<result>Based on my research of the official Claude Code documentation, here are the answers to your questions:

## Key Findings

### 1. **Sessions are tied to individual Claude accounts**

From the documentation:
> "A session is a saved conversation tied to a project directory."

Sessions are **account-specific**. Each Claude Code surface (web, CLI, desktop, VS Code) maintains its own separate session history. When you sign in to claude.ai/code, you authenticate with a single Claude account, and sessions created under that account are stored in "the sidebar at claude.ai/code" as personal sessions.

**Source**: [sessions.md](https://code.claude.com/docs/en/sessions.md) and [claude-code-on-the-web.md](https://code.claude.com/docs/en/claude-code-on-the-web.md)

### 2. **There is NO feature for transferring sessions or chat history between accounts**

The documentation does not mention any capability to transfer sessions, migrate chat history, or share the actual conversation data between two different Claude accounts. This is by design—sessions are fundamentally tied to the account that created them.

### 3. **Team/Organization accounts have limited session *visibility* sharing, not ownership transfer**

For **Claude for Teams or Enterprise** accounts only:
- You can set session visibility to **"Team"** to make a session visible to other members of your organization
- Recipients can **view** the shared session (read-only access to the conversation and changes)
- Their view does not update in real-time; they see the latest state when they open the link
- **Critically**: The session is still "owned" by the original creator's account. Each team member maintains their own account within the organization, their own session list, and their own set of environments

For **Max/Pro accounts**:
- Only **"Public"** sharing is available (visible to any logged-in claude.ai user)
- No team-level sharing

**Source**: [claude-code-on-the-web.md, "Share sessions" section](https://code.claude.com/docs/en/claude-code-on-the-web.md)

### 4. **Environments (cloud session configurations) are account-specific and not directly shareable**

"Environments" in Claude Code on the web are configurations (network access, environment variables, setup scripts) tied to your account. They are **not**:
- Transferable to another account
- Directly shared across accounts
- Part of a persistent "project" that multiple accounts can own

Each account creates its own environments at claude.ai/code. When you submit a task, it runs in an environment *your account owns*.

**Source**: [web-quickstart.md, "Create your environment" section](https://code.claude.com/docs/en/web-quickstart.md)

### 5. **Git/GitHub is where persisted work actually lives**

This is the critical point for your question: **The actual source code exists in the git repository (GitHub/GitLab/etc), not in the Claude Code session.**

- Sessions are ephemeral conversations. They don't persist the code changes themselves.
- What persists is: commits pushed to git branches, pull requests created on GitHub, and the actual code in the repository
- Any Claude account with access to the GitHub repository can:
  - Create new Claude Code sessions working on that repo
  - Review commits and PRs from other accounts
  - Teleport web sessions to the CLI (requires being authenticated to the *same* claude.ai account that created the web session)

This means if you want your work available across two separate Claude accounts, **you must rely on git**. The work itself (code changes) lives in the repository, not in Claude Code's session storage.

**Source**: [claude-code-on-the-web.md](https://code.claude.com/docs/en/claude-code-on-the-web.md) and [sessions.md, "Export and locate session data" section](https://code.claude.com/docs/en/sessions.md)

---

## The realistic, officially-supported way to share work across two Claude accounts:

1. **Push your commits to a shared git repository** (GitHub/GitLab/etc.)
2. **Ensure both Claude accounts have GitHub access** to that repository
3. **Each account can create independent Claude Code sessions** working on the same repo
4. **Each account maintains its own session history**, but all the actual code changes are in git and visible to both

You **cannot**:
- Transfer a session from one account to another
- Share environments across accounts
- Have a second account "take over" a session created by the first account
- Have shared chat history between two separate Claude accounts (outside of Team visibility, which is read-only viewing)

---

## What the docs don't explicitly cover:

I could not find official documentation describing:
- Whether organizations can enable a "shared workspace" mode where team members have collective project ownership
- How shared repositories are managed at the organization level for Claude Code
- Advanced team collaboration features beyond session visibility

If you need team-level project management or shared account features, I'd recommend contacting Anthropic support or your Claude for Teams/Enterprise admin, as those features may exist but aren't detailed in the public docs.

**Documentation sources:**
- [claude-code-on-the-web.md](https://code.claude.com/docs/en/claude-code-on-the-web.md) — Session sharing, environments
- [sessions.md](https://code.claude.com/docs/en/sessions.md) — Session fundamentals
- [web-quickstart.md](https://code.claude.com/docs/en/web-quickstart.md) — Environment setup
- [authentication.md](https://code.claude.com/docs/en/authentication.md) — Account authentication</result>
<usage><subagent_tokens>61230</subagent_tokens><tool_uses>10</tool_uses><duration_ms>243291</duration_ms></usage>
</task-notification>

addedBlocks:
## Figma
The official Figma MCP server. Use this server whenever the user wants to create, generate, edit, implement, or sync any design, UI, screen, component, mockup, or visual — in Figma, FigJam, Figma Make, or Figma Slides — and whenever the user mentions Figma or provides a figma.com URL.

This server bridges code and design in both directions, and supports designing from scratch using existing design systems and codebases.

CAPABILITIES:
- Read designs FROM Figma into code (get_design_context, get_screenshot, get_metadata, get_figjam)
- Write designs INTO Figma from code, intent, or existing components (use_figma, generate_figma_design, create_new_file, upload_assets)
- Bridge code and design via Code Connect (get_code_connect_map, add_code_connect_map)
- Create diagrams and FigJam content (generate_diagram, get_figjam)

WHEN TO USE THESE TOOLS:
- The user wants to create, mock up, or generate any UI, screen, component, or design — even if Figma isn't named
- The user wants to implement a design as code (design-to-code)
- The user wants to push a page, view, or component into Figma (code-to-design)
- The user wants to update, sync, or edit an existing Figma file
- The user shares a figma.com URL
- The user wants to build or extend a design system, design tokens, or component library
- The user wants to create a diagram in FigJam

SKILLS (Prefer the skills shipped with the Figma plugin on the agent being used. If no Figma plugin is installed, use the skills served by the Figma MCP resource):
- /figma-use — MANDATORY before calling use_figma; fallback: skill://figma/figma-use/SKILL.md
- /figma-generate-design — for translating an app page or layout into Figma; fallback: skill://figma/figma-generate-design/SKILL.md
- /figma-generate-library — for building a design system in Figma from code; fallback: skill://figma/figma-generate-library/SKILL.md
- /figma-code-connect — for mapping Figma components to codebase components; fallback: skill://figma/figma-code-connect/SKILL.md
- /figma-use-figjam — for FigJam-specific use_f… [truncated]

## Gamma
Gamma creates AI-powered presentations, documents, webpages, and social posts. Only include optional parameters when the user explicitly requests or clearly implies them. Gamma has intelligent defaults - when in doubt, omit. When uncertain about user preferences, ask rather than assume. The read_gamma tool can read the content of an existing Gamma when given its file ID. Existing gammas have a type: "template" or "regular". Only templates can be used with generate_from_template; use get_gammas with type="template" to discover valid templates. Non-template IDs are not valid inputs to generate_from_template. If generate_from_template reports that an ID is inaccessible or not a template, find a valid template with get_gammas(type="template"). If the user's intent is to create a new gamma, but not by using an existing template, then use the generate tool. IMPORTANT: The tools provided by this server cannot edit or modify existing gammas. Do not suggest that you can make changes, adjustments, or refinements to a generated gamma. Instead, let the user know they can customize their gamma directly in the Gamma editor.

## github
The GitHub MCP Server provides tools to interact with GitHub platform.

Tool selection guidance:
	1. Use 'list_*' tools for broad, simple retrieval and pagination of all items of a type (e.g., all issues, all PRs, all branches) with basic filtering.
	2. Use 'search_*' tools for targeted queries with specific criteria, keywords, or complex filters (e.g., issues with certain text, PRs by author, code containing functions).

Context management:
	1. Use pagination whenever possible with batches of 5-10 items.
	2. Use minimal_output parameter set to true if the full information is not needed to accomplish a task.

Tool usage guidance:
	1. For 'search_*' tools: Use separate 'sort' and 'order' parameters if available for sorting results - do not include 'sort:' syntax in query strings. Query strings should contain only search criteria (e.g., 'org:google language:python'), not sorting instructions. Always call 'get_me' first to understand current user permissions and context. ## Issues

Check 'list_issue_types' first for organizations to use proper issue types. Use 'search_issues' before creating new issues to avoid duplicates. Always set 'state_reason' when closing issues. ## Pull Requests

PR review workflow: Always use 'pull_request_review_write' with method 'create' to create a pending review, then 'add_comment_to_pending_review' to add comments, and finally 'pull_request_review_write' with method 'submit_pending' to submit the review for complex reviews with line-specific comments.

Before creating a pull request, search for pull request templates in the repository. Template files are called pull_request_template.md or they're located in '.github/PULL_REQUEST_TEMPLATE' directory. Use the template content to structure the PR description and then call create_pull_request tool.

content.text:
Разобрался. Давайте честно: **того, что вы хотите — «переключить второй аккаунт и увидеть те же чаты» — в Claude Code напрямую нет.** Вот почему и что делать вместо этого.

## Как это устроено

- **Чаты (сессии) привязаны к конкретному аккаунту.** Сессия — это сохранённый разговор, привязанный к вашей учётке. Второй аккаунт видит свой собственный список сессий и не может «унаследовать» ваши. Функции переноса истории чатов между двумя аккаунтами не существует.
- **Окружения (environments) — тоже per-account.** Настройки проекта в облаке (сеть, переменные, setup-скрипты) принадлежат аккаунту, который их создал, и не передаются другому.
- **Главное:** реальная работа живёт **не в чате, а в git-репозитории.** Чат эфемерный — в нём переписка. Сам код сохраняется в коммитах и ветках на GitHub. Это и есть то, что действительно можно «разделить» между аккаунтами.

## Что реально можно сделать

**Вариант 1 — через git (правильный и поддерживаемый способ для проектов).**
1. Пушите свои коммиты в общий GitHub-репозиторий (у вас это `alexautorental/panamera-studio`).
2. Дайте второму аккаунту доступ к этому репозиторию на GitHub.
3. Второй аккаунт заходит в claude.ai/code, подключает тот же репозиторий и **продолжает работу с того же кода**. Наработки (код, ветки, PR) будут видны обоим.

⚠️ При этом переписка чатов не перенесётся — но весь код и состояние проекта будут.

**Вариант 2 — чтобы «расшарить» именно чат (только просмотр).**
Если у вас **Teams/Enterprise**, конкретную сессию можно сделать видимой команде (visibility → «Team»): другой участник откроет ссылку и увидит разговор и изменения. Но это **read-only просмотр**, а не владение — он не сможет продолжать её как свою.
На **Pro/Max** доступен только «Public»-шаринг (виден любому залогиненному пользователю), тоже только просмотр.

## Мой совет

Если оба аккаунта — **ваши**, самый чистый путь: работайте через один общий GitHub-репозиторий и давайте обоим аккаунтам к нему доступ. Тогда «наработки» (проекты) синхронны всегда, а история чатов просто будет отдельной в каждом аккаунте — это ограничение обойти нельзя.

Хотите, уточню один момент, чтобы дать более точную инструкцию:

- Это **два ваших личных аккаунта** (например, два email), или речь про **командную подписку** (Teams)?
- Что важнее сохранить между аккаунтами — **проекты/код** или именно **история переписки в чатах**?

Если важна именно единая история чатов — то, к сожалению, единственный по-настоящему работающий вариант это **пользоваться одним аккаунтом** (просто логиниться им на обоих устройствах/в обоих приложениях). Разные аккаунты = разные истории, это by design.

lastPrompt: Я вот хочу, чтобы другой аккаунт Cloud Code работал с теми же тоже мой второй, работал с теми же вот наработками, что в этом Cloud Code. Как это сделать? С теми же проектами, с теми же чатами. Если я…

content:
Это два ваших личных аккаунта (например, два email), или речь про командную подписку (Teams)?
Что важнее сохранить между аккаунтами — проекты/код или именно история переписки в чатах? 

Да это оба моих аккаунта, не командный. Важно сохранить и то, и другое. Тогда нужно весь клот-код запушить туда в Панамеру на GitHub, чтобы я залогинился сейчас с новым аккаунтом, и чтобы он всё-всё то же самое видел и смог восстановить переписку. Ну, по крайней мере, не визуально, а для себя.

Что важнее сохранить между аккаунтами — проекты/код или именно история переписки в чатах? 

Да это оба моих аккаунта, не командный. Важно сохранить и то, и другое. Тогда нужно весь клот-код запушить туда в Панамеру на GitHub, чтобы я залогинился сейчас с новым аккаунтом, и чтобы он всё-всё то же самое видел и смог восстановить переписку. Ну, по крайней мере, не визуально, а для себя.

content.text:
Понял: два ваших личных аккаунта, и нужно сохранить **и код, и переписку**. Отлично — это выполнимо. Идея правильная: код уже уходит в git, а историю чатов можно **выгрузить в файлы и закоммитить в тот же репозиторий**. Тогда новый аккаунт склонирует Панамеру и Claude там сможет прочитать эти транскрипты и восстановить контекст «для себя» (не как красивый чат в интерфейсе, а как читаемые файлы).

Давайте я сначала найду, где в этом окружении лежат данные сессий/чатов.

content.text: Мы уже на нужной ветке, дерево чистое. Теперь выгружу историю чата в репозиторий в читаемом виде. Напишу скрипт, который превратит транскрипт в аккуратный Markdown (не выгружая все 90 КБ ко мне в контекст).

input.command:
mkdir -p /home/user/panamera-studio/docs/claude-sessions
cp /root/.claude/projects/-home-user-panamera-studio/c810ce71-594c-5291-94ff-5a2cc7ccbec0.jsonl \
   /home/user/panamera-studio/docs/claude-sessions/session-c810ce71.raw.jsonl
echo "copied raw jsonl:"
wc -l /home/user/panamera-studio/docs/claude-sessions/session-c810ce71.raw.jsonl
input.description: Create sessions dir and copy raw transcript
